text: Robotic Right Hemicolectomy on a Da Vinci X System. Presented by Dr. Somshekhar SP, Head of the Department and Consultant Surgical Oncologist, Manipal Comprehensive Cancer Center, Bangalore.

A forty nine year old male with a ECOG of zero presented with loss of weight and generalized weakness since six months and change in the bowel habit since three months. On examination he had pallor. Abdominal examination was not significant. Hemoglobin on presentation was seven point two.

On imaging, the ultrasound of the abdomen showed a mass lesion in the right iliac fossa arising from the ascending colon. CT scan showed a circumferential thickening of ascending colon with full thickness wall involvement of three point five centimeters and length of the involved segment around seven centimeters with luminal narrowing and fat stranding. There was no evidence of any ascites or metastasis. Serum CEA was one point six. Colonoscopy confirmed the findings with lumen compromised.

Biopsy revealed it to be a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma grade two with clinical stage as three B. Robotic right hemicolectomy with ileotransverse anastomosis was planned on the Da Vinci X system.

The surgeon performs a robotic right hemicolectomy, beginning with a thorough inspection of the abdomen. The hepatic flexure of the colon is mobilized, and the vascular pedicles, including the right branch of the middle colic artery, right colic artery, and ileocolic artery, are identified and ligated. The colon is mobilized from the right lateral parietal wall, with identification and preservation of the right ureter. Careful dissection of the duodenum is performed, followed by the transaction of the terminal ileum and transverse colon with adequate margins, concluding with an ileotransverse anastomosis.

The patient is positioned supine for robotic docking. The camera port is placed in the subumbilical line to the left, while robotic port R-I is positioned in the supraumbilical region below the costal arch. Robotic port R-II is located in the suprapubic region on the left side, and R-III is in the right iliac fossa. The assistant port is triangulated between R-I and R-II.

The operating room setup for the robotic right hemicolectomy is shown, with the instrument cart positioned at the patient's right shoulder. The assistant and scrub nurse are located to the patient's left.

The surgeon begins the dissection using hot shears at port R-I, a vessel sealer at port R-II, and a tip-up prograsp at port R-III. The hepatic flexure of the colon is visualized and mobilized as part of the initial dissection phase.

The surgeon begins dissection at the right ascending colon, following the white line of Toldt, and proceeds to medially mobilize the ascending colon. The hepatic flexure is mobilized first, with the underlying duodenum becoming visible as the dissection progresses towards the right lateral parietal wall.
The surgeon meticulously dissects around the duodenum using monopolar hot shears, taking care to avoid iatrogenic injury during the procedure.

The right branch of the middle colic vein is visualized, with the duodenum and head of the pancreas clearly visible below. The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the greater omentum from the mesocolon, revealing the branching pattern of the middle colic vein.

The surgeon separates the cecum from the white line of Toldt in the right iliac fossa, identifying the right-sided gonadal vessels and ureter. The dissection progresses cranially, mobilizing the terminal ileum. The cecum and appendix are lifted for better visualization.

The surgeon flips the bowel to locate the ileocolic pedicle, which is then ligated using a vessel sealer. Subsequently, the right colic pedicle is ligated, and the right branch of the middle colic pedicle is brought into view and similarly ligated with the vessel sealer.

The surgeon lifts the entire transverse colon and proceeds with dissection towards the left side, mobilizing the splenic flexure of the colon. This mobilization reduces tension on the anastomosis, completing the mobilization from the terminal ileum to the splenic flexure.
The surgeon makes a right-sided lower transverse abdominal incision and uses a wound guard to carefully extract the specimen, ensuring the tumor is not handled directly to prevent spillage.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side ileocolic anastomosis using a surgical stapler, aligning the ileum and colon for connection.

The total operative time in this case was two hundred and ten minutes with console time of sixty five minutes. Blood loss was around ninety ml and patient recovered uneventfully in the post operative period.

The final histopathology report was adenocarcinoma low-grade moderately differentiated of a size of ten by six by two centimeters. Margins were all uninvolved. Tumor invaded the subserosal adipose tissue but not extended to the serosal surface. There was no lymphovascular invasion. Lymph nodes were uninvolved. and around twenty-five lymph nodes were extracted. MSI being high, the patient had a stage IIa right-sided colonic malignancy, i.e. PT-N-M-M. Hence, patient was kept on observation and is on a regular follow-up.
